sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 63, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'DOWN': 'Moves block down by 5 rows (VERIFIED step 8)', 'LEFT': 'Moves block left by ~5 cols; works from rows 25-29 and rows 45-49; blocked from rows 30-44 (VERIFIED steps 6-22, 48-49)', 'RIGHT': 'Moves block right by ~5 cols (VERIFIED step 2)', 'UP': 'Moves block up by 5 rows (VERIFIED step 4)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'blue_templates': 'Two blue L-shapes in black frames (top-center and bottom-left) - purpose unknown', 'dark_gray_corridor': 'L-shaped passage inside gray room: vertical segment at cols 29-33, horizontal at rows 40-49 cols 14-33', 'orange_blue_block': 'Movable object: 4x2 orange on top + 4x3 blue below (total 4x5), moves in 5-cell steps', 'white_cross': 'Static reference marker at rows 31-32, cols 21-22; covered when block overlaps (step 53)'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'Yellow bar + red squares - likely timer/progress'}
- Cross-level notes: Block moves in 5-cell increments. Corridor has vertical segment (cols 29-33) and horizontal segment (rows 40-49, cols 14-33). LEFT works from rows 25-29 (top opening) and rows 45-49 (horizontal segment).
Current level:
- World model: Level 1. Block at rows 30-34, cols 19-22. Orange part at rows 30-31, cols 19-22; blue part at rows 32-34, cols 19-22. White cross no longer visible (covered by block). All 20 positions in 5x4 grid (rows 25-49, cols 19-38) have been tried without completing level.
- Goal model: Unclear - tried all reachable positions without completing level. Position-based goal hypothesis exhausted. Need entirely new hypothesis about what completes the level.
- Recent findings: ['Block can reach rows 30-34, cols 19-22 (step 53)', 'White cross covered when block at rows 30-34, cols 19-22', 'No white cross node in segmentation at step 53', 'All 20 positions in 5x4 grid tried: none complete level']
- Open questions: ['What completes the level if not position-based?', 'Do blue L-shape templates indicate the goal?', 'Is there an interaction mechanic not yet discovered?', "Could the goal involve the block's color or shape changing?"]
- Plan: Abandon position search. Try interacting with blue templates or white cross area differently. Consider if goal involves matching block shape to L-template.
- Strategy log: ['Tried all 20 positions in 5x4 grid (rows 25-49, cols 19-38): none complete level. Position-based goal hypothesis exhausted.', 'LEFT blocked from rows 30-44 corridor; only works from rows 25-29 and rows 45-49.']
- Failed probes: ['LEFT from cols 34-38 at rows 35-39: blocked (step 6)', 'LEFT from cols 34-38 at rows 40-44: blocked (step 11)', 'LEFT from cols 34-38 at rows 30-34: blocked (step 16)', 'RIGHT,LEFT,LEFT sequence: no extra movement (step 18)', 'All positions in rows 25-44, cols 19-38 tried: level not complete', 'Block at rows 45-49, cols 24-27: level not complete', 'Block at rows 45-49, cols 19-22: level not complete (step 49)', 'Block at rows 40-44, cols 19-22: level not complete (step 50)', 'Block at rows 35-39, cols 19-22: level not complete (step 51)', 'Block at rows 30-34, cols 19-22: level not complete (step 52)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 62 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 2.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the output, the orange boundary is still `[[30, 24], [30, 28], [31, 28], [31, 24]]` - same as before! But the board_changed was true for the RIGHT action. Wait, let me re-read.
The previous action was RIGHT, and board_changed was true. But the orange boundary is still at cols 24-27. This is confusing.
Wait, looking at the image again - the block appears to be in the same position as before. The orange part is at the same location. But the action result said board_changed: true.
Hmm, maybe the board changed but the block didn't move? Or maybe the orange part stayed but the blue part moved?
Actually, looking more carefully at the image, I notice the block now appears to be fully visible - both orange and blue parts are visible. In previous images, the blue part was sometimes hidden or merged with the corridor.
Wait, I think I'm misreading the boundary. The boundary `[[30, 24], [30, 28], [31, 28], [31, 24]]` shows rows 30-31 and cols 24-27. But this is just the orange part. The full block includes the blue part below.
Let me check if the blue part is now visible as a separate node. In the previous turn, the blue node found was the template at rows 11-13, not the block's blue part. This suggests the block's blue part was merged with the dark gray corridor.
Now, after RIGHT, the block should be at cols 29-32 (if RIGHT worked). But the orange boundary shows cols 24-27. This suggests RIGHT didn't actually move the block, or the block moved but the orange part stayed in the same place (which doesn't make sense).
Wait, I think I need to re-examine. The previous turn's output showed orange at `[[30, 24], [30, 28], [31, 28], [31, 24]]` and then RIGHT was executed with board_changed: true. Now the orange is still at the same boundary. This is strange.
Let me look at the image more carefully. The block appears to be at the same position - orange at rows 30-31, blue below. The white cross is to the left. The dark gray corridor is to the right.
I think the issue is that RIGHT didn't actually move the block, despite board_changed being true. Maybe some other part of the board changed (like the timer/HUD).
Let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to navigate through the corridor, let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is not about navigating through the corridor at all.
Looking at the blue L-shape templates:
1. Top-center: small blue L-shape in a black frame
2. Bottom-left: larger blue L-shape in a black frame
Maybe the goal is to match the block's shape to one of these templates? The block is 4x5 (orange 4x2 + blue 4x3), which is not an L-shape.
Or maybe the goal is to move the block to a specific position that matches the template's position?
Actually, let me reconsider the entire problem. The block moves in 5-cell increments. The corridor is an L-shape. Maybe the goal is to navigate the block through the entire L-shaped corridor, ending at the far left of the horizontal segment.
Let me try DOWN from the current position (rows 30-34, cols 24-27). If the block can move DOWN, it would go to rows 35-39. Then from there, maybe LEFT would work to move along the horizontal segment.
Wait, but earlier I tried DOWN from rows 35-39, cols 24-27 and it was blocked. Let me try DOWN from rows 30-34, cols 24-27.
Actually, looking at the image again, I see the block is now at rows 30-34 (after UP), and the orange part is visible. The blue part should be at rows 32-34. The dark gray corridor's vertical segment is at cols 29-33, rows 30-39. The block is at cols 24-27, which is to the left of the corridor.
I think the issue is that the block needs to be INSIDE the corridor to move through it. From cols 24-27, the block is outside the corridor. To enter the corridor, the block needs to move RIGHT to cols 29-32.
But earlier, RIGHT from rows 35-39, cols 24-27 was blocked. Now the block is at rows 30-34, cols 24-27. Let me try RIGHT again.
Wait, I already tried RIGHT and it said board_changed: true, but the orange boundary didn't change. Let me check if the blue part moved.
Actually, I think I need to look at this differently. Let me check the transition to see what actually changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after RIGHT action
if last_transition:
    before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
    after_seg = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation
    before_orange = [n for n in before_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'O']
    after_orange = [n for n in after_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'O']
    print(f"Before orange: {[n['boundary'] for n in before_orange]}")
    print(f"After orange: {[n['boundary'] for n in after_orange]}")
    
    # Check blue nodes
    before_blue = [n for n in before_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']
    after_blue = [n for n in after_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']
    print(f"Before blue nodes: {len(before_blue)}, boundaries: {[n['boundary'] for n in before_blue]}")
    print(f"After blue nodes: {len(after_blue)}, boundaries: {[n['boundary'] for n in after_blue]}")

# Current position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
orange_node = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'O'][0]
print(f"Current orange: {orange_node['boundary']}")

# Try RIGHT again - maybe it needs to be called again
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"DOWN": "Moves block down by 5 rows", "LEFT": "Moves block left by ~5 cols", "RIGHT": "Moves block right by ~5 cols", "UP": "Moves block up by 5 rows"}, "object_taxonomy": {"blue_templates": "Two blue L-shapes in black frames", "dark_gray_corridor": "L-shaped passage inside gray room", "orange_blue_block": "Movable object (4x5), moves in 5-cell steps", "white_cross": "Static marker"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "Yellow bar + red squares - timer"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": "Block moves in 5-cell increments"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 1. Block at rows 30-34, cols 24-27. RIGHT said board_changed but orange position unchanged - need to investigate.", "goal_model": "Navigate block through corridor", "recent_findings": ["RIGHT from rows 30-34, cols 24-27: board_changed but orange stayed at same position"], "open_questions": ["Did block actually move? What changed?"], "current_plan": "Check transition, try RIGHT again", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
